translation.content: We've been thinking about the music of our lifetime lately: mulling emergent icons, buy-on-sight staples and the cyclical nature of trends. All things bend around eventually, but if you lived through the mid-'00s the first time, it felt tricky to envision some specifics of those interim years making a second splash. More fool us. A bubblegum aesthetic best described as Topshop-core is in again; PinkPantheress has rekindled the insouciant spirit of Alright, Still-era Lily Allen; and dubstep is well and truly back. This resurged appetite for low-end has been a central storyline of the year, as you can hear in excellent 2025 highlights like Introspekt's earthen Moving The Center, Tracey's plunging "Sex Life" and more bassy picks in our Best of 2025 list. Alternately, you could have cupped your ear to the tremors rumbling from fields and clubs across the world and you'd find Mala hyping up crowds with gusto. Which makes landing a blockbuster RA Mix from the man himself serendipitous. The South Norwood sub sensei has anchored the movement for over 20 years now: first through the legendary DMZ, then DEEP MEDi, which he's run alongside Steph MEDi since 2006. A little like what Blue Beat was to Trojan, or Upsetter to Black Ark, the foundations DMZ laid down have been expanded by a label whose commitment to authentic, punchy, lineal sound system music has cultivated a hawklike fandom. (Six years of frothy debate and gentle ribbing in the run-up to the label's 100th release paints a picture of steep expectations.) But Mala's banner 2025 hasn't relied on the heads alone. Going off group chat exit polls, his fissuring basslines and barrel-chested vocals were the most buzzed-about at many events this summer. Even when he wasn't performing, the anthems that he and Coki pressed up two decades ago were usually the highlight of somebody else's set anyway. There are other qualities drawing people into his orbit alongside adrenaline, too—like pacifism, reinforced on Digital Mystikz's all-timer, or contemplation, inked on flyers beseeching the crowd to meditate on bass weight, then made tangible by long trips Mala took to Cuba and Peru in search of creative replenishment. In that spirit arrives a mix we've asked after for years. The Listening Session is rare on two counts. For starters, Mala is a conspicuous absence on most DJ series, and they're usually compact and high-energy. A nearly three-hour studio set—spaciously paced and laced with a combination of 2025 highlights, fresh dubplates and serious classics—is unheard of. Tracklist withheld for now, but we'll say this: on first run over the speakers at RA HQ, the phrase "best tune of all time incoming" rang out repeatedly. RA.1016 is the kind of document that jogs the memory back to when dubstep was a discrete enterprise, something you could only fleetingly access by, say, dialling into Youngsta on Sub FM, ripping 320s of "Circling" off long-forgotten blogs or hugging the back wall of Mass. Which, in service of thinking about the evolution of 21st-century electronic music, is pretty perfect really. – Gabriel Szatan 2025 has been a banner year for you. How's it felt from the inside? I've been very fortunate in the timing of my career. This year I've been all over; I just got back from India and had an incredible time. The time after lockdown had a bit of a stop-start rhythm, but I truly felt this year everything swung back into place. I have always felt aware that my responsibility as a producer, an artist, a DJ, is to try and offer some sense of discovery and challenge to my audience, and to make sure that the history of sound system music is still within what I do. When I look back on the music that influenced me over the years—like Nitin Sawhney and Talvin Singh, who are not "world music" in that sense, but definitely worldly, as well as Augustus Pablo, Sizzla, Capleton and King Tubby—that connection shines through. Nitin Sawhney's album Beyond Skin has probably influenced me the most in terms of my mindset, for what music can be, [so much so that] when I worked on Mala In Cuba with Gilles Peterson, it didn't feel alien to me. Working with musicians like Roberto Fonseca and Changito from Buena Vista Social Club, being in a foreign land and feeling really out of my depth—these things helped me grow and challenged not only my perception of who I am artistically, but also my audience's perception of me. Before Mala In Cuba, I'd only been making dubplates and releasing singles for the dance, you know? I didn't grow up listening to bands. I never aspired to be in a band and play an instrument. Even though we rolled together as DMZ, it's often been a really solo, even isolated creative process for most of my career. Going to Cuba, though, opened me up in a way that I'd never experienced before. It's no surprise we've been after a mix from you for a long time, and we're very pleased to make it happen. You've titled this The Listening Session, with a switch-up in pace as well as a lot of deeper classics. What was the gameplan? Yeah, I am sorry about not making it happen so far. We got there in the end. [Laughs] I find putting mixes together quite challenging, so there's not many of mine out there. The reaction of putting a product out there feels uncertain, so I politely decline most asks, even though I appreciate them. I do love playing music to an audience and sharing that live experience, but when I'm at home in my studio, I'm not in that mindset of tearing it out as if I'm in a dance. It's the same logic as with Mala In Cuba. The only reason that album exists is because I actually went to Cuba; I experienced it, lived it for a while and worked with the musicians, so I didn't have to fabricate a story for the concept of the record. ​​I've also always thought of a 90-minute set as my minimum timeframe. I really enjoyed two or three hour-long sets, or even longer, even though I'm usually allocated something more like 60 minutes on the road. It feels like in 2025, I've played more one hour slots than any other year, especially in the USA. There's something about that long form structure that allows me to play a way that feels more suited to both the music that I play and the approach that I want to take. It can take me 15 or 20 minutes to reset a room, depending on what's been played before me. Your festival sets this year have felt distinctly high-impact. Tell me more about how you're approaching those slots. Now more than ever, I see a knock-on effect of music festivals in clubs. You'll have genre-diverse lineups, and even sets which feature multiple genres, but if you've only got an hour to play, I question what that does for the DJ. Does it allow them to express and experiment within the full range of music that they want to play? Or do they feel obligated to play bangers, because they want to deliver within the time frame they're given? And on a deeper level, what does that do for music and culture? We do a dozen or so DEEP MEDi sessions per year and I love that we play the same genre of music from start to finish, giving it the space to incubate, allowing the producers and DJs to experiment. Part of being a DJ or selector is that you make the track you just played sound great because of the track you played before it, and it's a constant selection of making music sound great because of the way that music is being explored and structured. What I'm kind of seeing in a lot of spaces at the moment is that it's become more about pleasing rather than challenging an audience. I think me playing for almost three hours in this mix is a little bit of a reaction to the idea of "Let's bang out for an hour with all the tracks that we know will go off in the dance." I've always felt more aligned with DJs that play these long-form, expressive DJ sets—like Theo Parrish, Floating Points, Calibre, Benji B and Four Tet. The thing that really leaps out to me in this mix is the pacing. It's breathed new life into tunes I've heard a million times, as well as really bringing out the mood in new ones like "Destiny" by Sheba Q and Cid Poitier, which feels not a million miles from MJ Cole's remix of Carmen McRae now. When I was selecting music for this mix, I picked the "Sinners" record because it leans into the dub origins of dubstep—especially the dubstep that I come from—and a few others. After playing them, I was reminded that even though dubstep has bangers, from people like Skream, Coki and Benga, the music somehow still felt patient. Yes, DJs like Hatcha and N-Type were mixing very quickly, but there was still this patience to it; where the space within the music gives the track a chance to breathe, and did something for the live audience and the home listener. A key factor that really gives those early records weight is the way they were mastered and cut at Transition Mastering Studios. Jason Goz's role in all this is still really underrated. We [as DMZ] were really lucky, because Jason and Transition was the closest mastering house to us in South London. A lot of people used to go to Music House in Tottenham, but that was way too far for us. Jason cut dubs for Jah Shaka and for drum & bass artists, so he understood what we were doing. It felt right; he knows sound system music and electronic music, so we were the perfect match for cutting all of our music in that era. Only once you got into his network of trust, you were allowed into the upstairs office. That felt very special. The first time I went down there, I heard a Scottish accent and thought to myself, "ah that must be Kode9." We'd communicated a little bit over email but I wanted to introduce myself in person, to let him know my thanks for replying to my messages. We didn't know then what we know now, in terms of our technical ability, but if we had been all educated on that—our own engineers, to a degree—then the sound wouldn't be the same. It needed to be minimal, raw and earthy. Not having the ability to play CDs or upload something to SoundCloud meant that, before we went to Jason to spend our £30 for two tracks on a dubplate, we had to make very, very sure that what we were giving him was done to the absolute best of our ability. It was our own quality control check. Jason told me that "Anti War Dub" was one of the best mixes that I ever delivered to him back then; in terms of the balance, the tone, everything. I remember him always giving me props for that one in a way that he hadn't for the others, you know? We were our own songwriters, programmers and engineers back then. Those early DMZ staples carry a lot of heritage: as well as the 20th century model of cutting dubplates, a link back to the roots of sound system culture, and also some family connections too. "Anti War Dub" started out very differently in Jamaica, right? So: when Coki used to go to Jamaica, he would link up with people out there—relatives, people he knew—and would sometimes come back with different recordings. Coki had a version of "Anti War Dub," which was a dubwise reggae track, complete with verses and everything. Coki gave me that acapella and I played around with it. I chopped up four sentences from the original track, the bassline melody and the vocal by Spen G, and intended for it to be a 30-second interlude, you know? A year or so later, I pulled it back up and the beat came to mind straight away. I used stock plug-in sounds on Reason, no external samples or plug-ins. I read somewhere that people thought I'd sampled Bugz In The Attic, but not at all. I can actually pull up the file here and now; my old PC is still functional, miraculously, so I have the original Anti War Dub as it was when we took it to get pressed. This is all bring back a lot of memories. I lost my dad a couple of years ago. My dad came to DMZ at Mass in Brixton only once, and he was standing by the DJ booth when I dropped "Anti War Dub." I remember the audience reaction, and looking at my dad's face…in that moment, he understood what I was doing in that studio in our spare bedroom at home. He always believed in what I was doing. "Anti War Dub" reminds me of that: my dad felt it. Just finally: As we're running down our favourite music of the 21st century, I'm happy to let you know that "Anti War Dub" is number two on the tracks list. [Long pause] How frequencies resonate are kind of mysterious. To think of it there is... kind of beyond comprehension. You can't really get your head around something like that. I'm actually a bit speechless to be honest with you. Charts and awards have never been the goal, but I am deeply touched by this recognition. I'd also like to give thanks to the global underground music communities in their many shapes and forms as they continue to evolve and inspire.
translation.blurb: One of the defining producers of the 21st century steps up for a rare, era-spanning mix.
